message:
fix(fr-server): re-register replica sockets for WRITABLE after queueing replication writes, and propagate blocking-op errors

Two correctness fixes in the mio event loop.

1) Replication write readiness. `propagate_writes_to_replicas` was queueing
   bytes into `conn.write_buf` and inserting the token into `write_tokens`,
   but it was not telling mio that the replica socket now wants to be
   woken on WRITABLE. If the replica connection had only been registered
   for READABLE (the baseline after handshake), the write buffer would sit
   there until the next unrelated event, adding a round-trip of tail
   latency — or worse, stalling replication entirely in an idle cluster.

   Thread the `Poll` down into the function and call
   `poll.registry().reregister(&mut conn.stream, token,
   Interest::READABLE | Interest::WRITABLE)` at the moment we queue bytes.
   The main loop then gets the WRITABLE event on the next tick and flushes
   via the usual write path. The `let _ =` on the reregister is the same
   best-effort pattern used elsewhere in the file — a failed reregister
   would be surfaced by the subsequent read/write attempt anyway.

   The existing `propagate_writes_to_replicas` unit test is updated to
   construct a real `mio::Poll` and pass it through, proving the function
   is still callable from tests without a running socket.

2) Blocking op error propagation. In `try_fulfill_blocked`, three sites
   (BLPOP, BRPOP, BLMPOP) executed an inner frame against the runtime and
   then `continue`-d if the response was `RespFrame::Error(_)`. That was
   wrong: `continue` here meant "try the next key in the multi-key block",
   which silently hid the error from the client and could loop forever if
   every key raised the same structured error. The correct behavior is to
   return the error to the blocked caller immediately, which is what
   legacy Redis does. Replace the three `continue`s with `return
   Some(response);` so errors fall through the blocking machinery the same
   way successful values do.

Both fixes are on the strict-parity path between frankenredis and legacy
Redis; the replication-wakeup one in particular explains some of the
mysteriously large p99 tails seen in the baseline replication tests.
